title: Buttermilk scones, American-style
subtitle: Tall, flaky scones built on buttermilk
excerpt: American-style scones with buttermilk in place of milk, giving a tangy crumb and a taller rise. The dough is folded once or twice for layered flakiness. Twelve scones from the bowl in under thirty minutes.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): records buttermilk as the older liquid in tea-table scones (predating the milk-and-cream-of-tartar shortcut). USDA dairy bulletin (1930s, public domain): the acid in buttermilk reacts with the bicarbonate of soda to give the rise. The folding step (turn and fold the dough on itself to build layers) is documented in pre-1928 American household manuals as the path to a taller, flakier scone.

recipe.freezeNotes: Open-freeze unbaked scones on a tray; bag once solid; bake from frozen, adding 3 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cube and freeze the butter for 10 minutes before rubbing in if the kitchen is warm.

glossaryTerms.definition: After bringing the dough together, the slab is rolled or patted to a rectangle, folded into thirds like a letter, and turned ninety degrees. The fold creates layers of butter and dough that puff apart in the oven, giving a flakier crumb than a straight pat-and-cut scone. One or two folds is enough; more risks overworking the gluten.

content.text: The American scone is taller and flakier than its British cousin, and the difference comes from two things: buttermilk in place of milk, and a fold-and-turn step that builds layers in the dough. The buttermilk's acid reacts with the bicarbonate of soda for an extra lift; the folds give the layered crumb.
Serve warm with butter, or split and topped with cream cheese and smoked salmon for a savoury turn.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Whisk the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{caster-sugar}} in a wide bowl. Rub the {{unsalted-butter}} in with cold fingertips until the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs with pea-sized pieces of butter still visible. The bigger pieces give the flakiness.
Pour in the {{buttermilk}} and stir with a fork until the dough comes together; it will look rough. Tip onto a floured surface and pat to a rectangle about 2 cm thick.
like a letter, turn ninety degrees, and pat back to a 2 cm slab. Repeat the fold once more. Cut twelve rounds with a floured 5 cm cutter, straight down.

Set on a lined baking tray with 3 cm between scones. Brush with the beaten {{eggs}}. Bake for 12–14 minutes; the tops should be golden and the sides should show layered flakes. Cool on a rack 5 minutes before splitting.
